title: Coil-pot bowl in air-dry clay
subtitle: A rounded bowl built from rolled coils without a wheel or kiln
excerpt: Coil-building is the oldest method for making a rounded pot: coils of clay are stacked on a base and joined to raise walls of any height and curve. This project produces a small bowl using air-dry clay. No kiln and no wheel are needed. The bowl is decorative; air-dry clay is not waterproof.
sourceNotes: Coil-building technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rope of clay rolled to an even diameter, the building unit in coil-built pottery.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to join and trim.
Clay mixed with water to a thick cream consistency, used as an adhesive when joining clay pieces.

content.text: A
coil

-built bowl is among the oldest ceramic forms: coils stacked and joined, the walls raised by hand without a wheel. This version uses air-dry clay and requires no kiln. The finished bowl takes about two to three hours of active work spread over two days, as the walls need to stiffen between layers. The result is decorative; do not fill air-dry clay with water.

attrs.title: No kiln, not waterproof
attrs.body: Air-dry clay bowls are decorative. They will soften and eventually break down if left holding water. Use for dry items, small plants in a terracotta liner, or display.

content.text: What you need

items.name: Clay slurry (same clay mixed with water to thick cream)
Canvas board or wooden board for rolling

content.text: Building the bowl
Step 1: Make the slurry
Mix a tablespoon of clay scraps with water in a small jar until the consistency is thick cream. This is the
slurry
used to join every coil. Keep the jar covered between uses.
Step 2: Make the base

Roll a slab of clay 8 mm thick. Cut a circle about 12 cm in diameter using the rim of a mug as a guide. This is the base. Set it on the canvas board and cover the edges with a damp sponge to keep them workable while the base firms slightly, about 20 minutes.
Step 3: Roll the first coil and attach it to the base

Roll a coil 12 mm in diameter and long enough to go around the base circumference. Score the edge of the base where the coil will sit and apply slurry. Press the coil firmly onto the scored edge, matching the diameter of the base circle. Join the two ends of the coil by pressing them together diagonally, not straight across. Blend the coil into the base on the interior with a finger, working in short downward strokes from the coil into the base.
Step 4: Add subsequent coils

Score the top of the previous coil, apply slurry, and press the new coil on top. For a bowl that curves inward toward a rim, position each new coil slightly to the inside of the previous one. For a straight-sided pot, place each coil directly on top of the last. Blend each coil on the interior immediately after placement. Allow the walls to stiffen at
leather-hard
stage before adding more than three coils: wet walls collapse under their own weight.
Step 5: Smooth the exterior

Once all coils are placed and the bowl is at leather-hard, use a damp sponge to smooth the exterior surface. Work in circular strokes to blend the coil ridges. A wooden rib pressed against the outside of the wall and a finger inside can thin and smooth the wall further if needed. Leave the interior blended but not necessarily smooth: coil-building shows its construction as part of the form.
Step 6: Finish the rim

Trim the top edge level with the fettling knife, holding it horizontal. Compress the rim with a wet finger to round and seal it. A flat rim reads as controlled; a rough rim reads as unfinished. Allow to dry to bone-dry over one to two days, covered loosely with plastic to slow drying and avoid cracking.
Finishing

At bone-dry, sand any rough areas lightly with 120-grit sandpaper outdoors. Seal with a coat of PVA glue diluted 1:1 with water or a dedicated air-dry clay sealer. Two coats gives a light sheen and consolidates the surface. Paint with acrylic paints if desired, then apply a final sealant coat.
Variations
Leave the coil ridges visible on the exterior by smoothing only the interior: the ribbed surface is a valid design statement.
Use paper clay for larger bowls: the cellulose fibres knit the coils together more securely and reduce cracking at joins on taller walls.
Add a coiled foot ring by pressing a smaller coil around the base once the walls are leather-hard, blending it securely on both sides.
Common problems

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems in coil-pot building and how to address them.

items.cause: Too many coils added before the walls stiffened, or wall thickness is uneven.
items.fix: Add no more than three coils at a time before allowing the wall to reach leather-hard. Keep wall thickness consistent at around 8 mm.

items.symptom: Coils crack away from the base
items.cause: The base and the coils were at different drying stages when joined, or scoring was insufficient.
items.fix: Score both surfaces deeply and use plenty of slurry. Match the moisture level of the coil to the edge of the base before joining.

items.symptom: Rim cracks around its circumference as the bowl dries
items.cause: The rim is the thinnest part and dried fastest.
items.fix: Cover the rim with a damp strip of cloth or plastic while the base catches up in drying. Slow drying reduces stress.
